page_intro: The lab works in five areas, and they connect. Clinical data raises mechanical questions; the biomechanical models we build to answer them point to new techniques; those techniques generate new data; and the software we write moves all of it faster. Each area below lists representative work and the open problems we are taking on now.
a1_methods:
METHODS
Federated EHR analysis
Pharmacoepidemiology
Causal inference
Survival modeling
a1_p1: Randomized trials answer one question at a time and take years. Modern electronic health record networks hold the records of hundreds of millions of patients, enough to run carefully controlled analyses that would otherwise be impossible. We build those analyses for musculoskeletal disease.
a1_p2: The work is methodological as much as clinical: controlling confounding by indication, handling immortal-time and selection bias, and making federated estimates that hold up across institutions. Done well, it turns routine care data into hypotheses worth testing.

a1_studies.text: A medication-wide screen of 50 drugs for association with hip and knee osteoarthritis, using a federated network of routinely collected clinical data.
Post-menopausal hormone therapy and greater trochanteric pain syndrome: a retrospective cohort of 385,868 women, including the effect of initiation timing.

a2_methods:
METHODS
Finite element analysis
Patient-specific modeling
Image-based meshing
Constitutive modeling
a2_p1: Some questions are mechanical and cannot be answered by any trial: how a surgical construct shares load, where it concentrates stress, and where it will fail first. We build patient-specific finite element models from clinical imaging to answer them, then tie the predictions back to real outcomes.
a2_p2: Our current focus is the hip abductors. Tendon repairs fail often, and augmentation with a scaffold is increasingly common without a clear mechanical rationale. We model exactly how a scaffold redistributes load across the repair, patient by patient, to understand who benefits and why.
a2_fig_caption: Load to failure: native repair versus scaffold-augmented construct (schematic).

a2_studies.text: Load-sharing mechanics of scaffold augmentation in gluteus medius tendon repairs: a patient-specific finite element analysis.

a3_methods:
METHODS
Robotic arthroplasty data
Prediction vs ground truth
Computer vision
Registry linkage
a3_p1: Robotic platforms for joint replacement record every cut, gap, and alignment angle, an unusually rich, structured stream of intraoperative data. Most of it is never analyzed. We treat the robot's plan as a prediction and test it against what the surgeon actually finds and does.
a3_p2: The first results are already pointing somewhere useful: as a patient's native coronal deformity increases, the platform systematically underpredicts the medial gap, with direct implications for how the knee is balanced. Measuring and correcting that gap is what this program works on.
a3_fig_caption: Interactive and schematic. Drag the slider to see the measured gap diverge from the robot's plan as native deformity grows.

a3_studies.text: Increasing native coronal deformity is associated with medial gap underprediction in robotic-assisted total knee arthroplasty.

a4_title: AI agents in the clinical workflow
a4_methods:
METHODS
LLM & agent engineering
Retrieval & evidence synthesis
FHIR / clinical data
Evaluation & safety
a4_p1: The lab runs on software it builds. We design agentic systems to do real work in a surgical department: synthesizing evidence, assembling cohorts from messy clinical data, and drafting structured documentation. Because they operate where care happens, we hold them to a clinical standard, evaluated, audited, and measured against the task they claim to do.
a4_p2: This is the through-line of the whole lab. The same agents that accelerate a literature review or a database build are prototypes for tools that could one day sit inside the clinical workflow itself. We are interested in both: the research accelerant and the eventual product.

a4_studies.text: Agents that turn intraoperative and EHR data into registry-ready, auditable surgical documentation, evaluated like any other clinical intervention.

a5_methods:
METHODS
Surgical anatomy
Approach & construct design
Cadaveric & clinical validation
Outcome measurement
a5_p1: This program takes anatomic and biomechanical insight all the way to technique: new approaches, instruments, and repair strategies, then measures whether they actually help.
a5_p2: Recent work includes a novel superior approach to total hip arthroplasty and a re-examination of how partial, concealed abductor tears are classified, an under-recognized lesion that standard descriptions miss. The aim is a technique that changes practice, supported by data.

a5_studies.text: The Superior Transverse Anatomic Reconstruction (STAR) approach in total hip arthroplasty.
Partial subsurface detachment of the hip abductors: a concealed lesion described across nine studies under competing names.

open_problems.title: Predicting which abductor tendon repairs fail
open_problems.desc: Gluteus medius repairs fail at meaningful rates, and outcomes vary widely. Using imaging, intraoperative findings, and longitudinal outcomes from our repair cohort, the goal is a model that identifies repairs at high risk of failure and the factors that drive it.
open_problems.needs: DATA: IMAGING, OPERATIVE, OUTCOMES · METHODS: ML, SURVIVAL ANALYSIS

open_problems.title: From intraoperative data to a structured operative record
open_problems.desc: Robotic platforms log detailed data for every case, but little of it reaches surgical registries because the translation is manual. The problem is an agentic pipeline that converts intraoperative and EHR data into structured, auditable documentation a surgeon would sign.
open_problems.needs: DATA: ROBOTIC LOGS, EHR · METHODS: LLM AGENTS, FHIR

open_problems.title: Separating cause from association in drug-osteoarthritis screens
open_problems.desc: A medication-wide screen surfaces many drugs associated with osteoarthritis. Association is not cause. The work is to take the strongest signals and test them with negative controls, target-trial emulation, and sensitivity analysis to see what holds.
open_problems.needs: DATA: FEDERATED EHR · METHODS: CAUSAL INFERENCE, PHARMACOEPIDEMIOLOGY

open_problems.title: A fast surrogate for patient-specific finite element analysis
open_problems.desc: A full finite element model of a repair construct takes hours to build and solve. A learned surrogate that predicts the stress field from imaging in seconds, accurately enough to inform a decision, would make this usable in clinic.
open_problems.needs: DATA: IMAGING, FE MODELS · METHODS: SURROGATE MODELING, GEOMETRY PROCESSING

open_problems.title: Correcting the robot's gap prediction across deformity
open_problems.desc: Robotic knee replacement underpredicts the medial gap as native coronal deformity increases. The problem is to characterize that error across the full deformity range and build a correction a surgeon can use intraoperatively.
open_problems.needs: DATA: ROBOTIC ARTHROPLASTY · METHODS: MODELING, DATA SCIENCE

open_problems.title: A reproducible classification for concealed abductor tears
open_problems.desc: Partial, subsurface abductor tears sit under intact superficial fibers and are described in the literature under competing names. The work is an imaging-based, reproducible classification, ideally with automated segmentation, that the field can use consistently.
open_problems.needs: DATA: MRI · METHODS: SEGMENTATION, CLINICAL RESEARCH
